original_abstract:
Silver-copper (AgCu) bimetallic catalysts are highly regarded for their ability to synergistically improve the electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide (CO2RR) into multicarbon products. However, the structural and chemical durability of these materials under operating conditions remains poorly understood, making it difficult to identify active sites or design long-term stable catalysts. In this study, we synthesized both phase-blended (alloyed) and phase-separated AgCu nanoparticles to systematically track their evolution during CO2RR. 

Using time-sequential scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and elemental mapping, we demonstrate that copper possesses significant mobility under reaction conditions. Cu species tend to leach from the catalyst interior, migrate to the surface, and eventually detach to form separate agglomerates. Crucially, we found that regardless of the initial configuration, both catalyst types evolve toward a common thermodynamic state characterized by distinct phase separation into Ag-rich (Ag0.88Cu0.12) and Cu-rich (Ag0.05Cu0.95) domains. Lead underpotential deposition (Pb-UPD) further confirms that this phase separation occurs on the catalyst surface, highlighting the importance of the resulting Ag-Cu heterojunctions in driving catalytic activity. Additionally, operando high-energy resolution fluorescence detector (HERFD) X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) revealed that copper is fully reduced to a metallic state during electrolysis and that the presence of silver helps suppress the re-oxidation of copper upon air exposure. These findings provide a detailed roadmap of the chemical and structural transformations of AgCu electrocatalysts, offering critical insights for the rational design of robust bimetallic systems for carbon utilization.
humanized_abstract:
Silver–copper (AgCu) bimetallic catalysts have been shown to enhance electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide into multicarbon products through synergetic effect. The stability of the structure and chemical compositions of the catalysts under working conditions is not fully understood, which obstructs the location of active centers and preparation of stable catalysts. In this work, phase-blended (alloyed) and phase-separated silver and copper nanoparticles were prepared and used in situ to study the stability of the bimetallic nanoparticles during CO2RR.

Using time-sequential scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and elemental mapping we demonstrate that copper exhibits a high mobility under catalytic reaction conditions. Cu is leached from the catalyst body, transported to the surface and then is precipitated out as separate Cu aggregates. Irrespectively of the initial state of the catalyst, both catalyst structures evolve towards a final thermodynamically stable configuration, featuring a clear phase separation into Ag-rich (Ag0.88Cu0.12) and Cu-rich (Ag0.05Cu0.95) domains. Using lead underpotential deposition (Pb-UPD) we confirm that the phase separation occurs at the catalyst surface and points to the crucial role of the formed Ag-Cu heterojunctions for catalytic performance. By means of operando high-energy resolution fluorescence detector (HERFD) X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) we demonstrate by that copper is fully reduced during electrolysis and that silver acts as a prevention measure for the re-oxidation of copper species upon exposure to air. Thus, our study is a “recipe book” of what happens to AgCu electrocatalysts under catalytic conditions”, essential for the design of more stable bimetallic catalysts for carbon conversion reactions.

document.output:

document.input:
